coredata.dc:title: Use of carbon dots to enhance UV-blocking of transparent nanocellulose films
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Xin
Zhao, Yafei
Jiang, Yaoquan
Miao, Miao
Cao, Shaomei
Fang, Jianhui
coredata.dc:description: High-efficient transparent UV-blocking nanocellulose (NC) films were successfully assembled by pressured-extrusion of the composites of carbon dots (CDs), 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) radical mediated oxidized nanocellulose (ONC) and ZnO nanostructures. ONC nanofibrils were firstly extracted from bamboo fibers and subsequently prepared by applying TEMPO oxidation. The as-obtained CDs-ONC-ZnO films exhibited high visible light transparency, excellent thermal stability and enhanced UV-blocking properties. Compared to the previously designed NC-ZnO films, CDs-ONC-ZnO films presented significant increase of UV-blocking ratio (UVR) with the same amounts of ZnO. Moreover, the UVR of CDs-ONC-s-ZnO film with 4wt% sheet-like ZnO (s-ZnO) at 300nm and 225nm is 92.74% and 98.99%, better than the same condition of CDs-ONC-b-ZnO film added with belt-like ZnO (b-ZnO) and CDs-ONC-p-ZnO film added with commercial particulate ZnO (p-ZnO). An interesting discovery is that when adding 4wt% p-ZnO, the UVR of CDs-ONC-p-ZnO film is very close to the value of NC-s-ZnO film with the same amount of s-ZnO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bamboo
Nanocellulose
Carbon dots
Transparent UV-blocking film
Thermal stability

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of ONC (a) before and (b) after homogenization; TEM image of CDs-ONC (c), HRTEM image of CDs-ONC (d).
Digital images of CDs-ONC-ZnOx hybrid films with different amounts of ZnO (x=0, 1, 2, 3 and 4wt%) and the corresponding SEM images, (a) s-ZnO, (b) b-ZnO and (c) p-ZnO.
XRD patterns of (a) CDs-ONC-s-ZnOx, (b) CDs-ONC-b-ZnOx and (c) bCDs-ONC-p-ZnOx films with different amounts of ZnO nanostructures (x=0, 1, 2wt%).
FT-IR spectra of ONC, CDs-ONC, CDs-ONC-p-ZnO4, CDs-ONC-b-ZnO4 and CDs-ONC-s-ZnO4 films.
UV–vis spectra of (a and d) CDs-ONC-s-ZnOx, (b and e) CDs-ONC-b-ZnOx and (c and f) CDs-ONC-p-ZnOx films with different amounts of ZnO nanostructures (x=0, 1, 2, 3, 4wt%).
TG curves of CDs-ONC, CDs-ONC-s-ZnO, CDs-ONC-b-ZnO and CDs-ONC-p-ZnO hybrid films.
Digital pictures of (a and b) PET film and (c and d) CDs-ONC-ZnO film at ambient temperature and after heated to 300°C for 20min, respectively.
The transmittance, Visible-shielding Ratio (VR) and UV-blocking Ratio (UVR) of CDs-ONC-s-ZnO, CDs-ONC-b-ZnO and CDs-ONC-p-ZnO films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The transmittance of CDs-ONC-s-ZnO, CDs-ONC-b-ZnO and CDs-ONC-p-ZnO hybrid films at 550nm were labeled as T(%)-s550, T(%)-b550 and T(%)-p550, respectively. The VR(%) and UVR(%) at 300nm, 225nm and 550nm were evaluated by using Eq. (1) (Li et al., 2011).

head.ce:title: Use of carbon dots to enhance UV-blocking of transparent nanocellulose films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Nano Science and Technology, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Nano Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-efficient transparent UV-blocking nanocellulose (NC) films were successfully assembled by pressured-extrusion of the composites of carbon dots (CDs), 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) radical mediated oxidized nanocellulose (ONC) and ZnO nanostructures. ONC nanofibrils were firstly extracted from bamboo fibers and subsequently prepared by applying TEMPO oxidation. The as-obtained CDs-ONC-ZnO films exhibited high visible light transparency, excellent thermal stability and enhanced UV-blocking properties. Compared to the previously designed NC-ZnO films, CDs-ONC-ZnO films presented significant increase of UV-blocking ratio (UVR) with the same amounts of ZnO. Moreover, the UVR of CDs-ONC-s-ZnO film with 4wt% sheet-like ZnO (s-ZnO) at 300nm and 225nm is 92.74% and 98.99%, better than the same condition of CDs-ONC-b-ZnO film added with belt-like ZnO (b-ZnO) and CDs-ONC-p-ZnO film added with commercial particulate ZnO (p-ZnO). An interesting discovery is that when adding 4wt% p-ZnO, the UVR of CDs-ONC-p-ZnO film is very close to the value of NC-s-ZnO film with the same amount of s-ZnO.

ce:section.ce:para: As the concerns of human health care continue increase, particularly focusing on human skin, eyes, immune system and biological systems, UV-blocking materials development have been propelled with a quite fast pace (Gause & Chauhan, 2016; Qu et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2013). The UV radiation coming from space or environment around human beings has resulted in an ever increasing damage, which is the most prominent and ubiquitous (Sirerol et al., 2015). Therefore, the exploring of high efficient UV-blocking materials attracts recently considerable research interests. As one of the promising UV-absorbing materials, the typical II–VI semiconductor ZnO has gained substantial interest in the research community (Sparks et al., 2013), due to its efficient absorbance UV radiation coming from the atmospheric ozone layer. Additionally, ZnO possesses large exaction-binding energy (60meV) and wide band gap (3.37eV), which has attracted tremendous interest owing to its remarkable environmentally friendly nature and chemical stability under exposure to both high temperatures and UV radiation (Chen et al., 2010; Chen, Ling, Sun, & Sun, 2011; Utama et al., 2012). ZnO with different shape of structure is widely used, including photocatalysts, photoelectric conversion, antibacterial and bactericide, UV-blocking materials, photoluminescence materials and so on (Applerot, Perkas, Amirian, Girshevitz, & Gedanken, 2009; El-Hady, Farouk, & Sharaf, 2013; He, Li, Wang, Su, & Tong, 2011; Pan et al., 2012; Shahabi-Ghahfarrokhi, Khodaiyan, Mousavi, & Yousefi, 2015; Zhang, Liu, Morikawa, & Chen, 2013). Although the UV-blocking films based on polymer substrates combined with semiconductor had been developed for UV protection (Murariu et al., 2011; Tu et al., 2010), a vital limitation is that the plastic films cannot endure high temperature. So scientists have been trying to find new alternatives (Alimohammadi, Gashti, & Shamei, 2012; Alimohammadi, Gashti, & Shamei, 2013; Han & Yu, 2006). Compared with petroleum-based plastic films, cellulosic substrates exhibit enhanced thermal stability and pronounced UV protective properties by coating graphene as a novel UV blocking agent, the inorganic/organic hybrid coating method provides a promising tool for achieving multifunctional cellulosic fabrics (Nooralian, Gashti, & Ebrahimi, 2016). As a transparent cellulosic substrate, nanocellulose (NC) films have obtained tremendous attention for a wide spectrum of products and materials in daily life, playing the role of the most abundant renewable polymer resource available today (Matsumoto & Kitaoka, 2015; Österberg et al., 2013; Shimizu, Saito, Fukuzumi, & Isogai, 2014; Wang, Tian, Ras, & Ikkala, 2015). Due to their unsurpassed and quintessential physical-chemical properties, renewability, sustainability and thermostability, it is obvious that the novel NC films are excellent candidates to supersede traditional plastic films for UV protection. By incorporating with lignin, natural UV-absorbent and visible-transparent NC films could be obtained (Hambardzumyan, Foulon, Chabbert, & Aguié-Béghin, 2012). Hambardzumyan et al. (2015) adopted Fention’s reagent reaction to supports better dispersion of lignin molecules on NC surface, thus improving the UV-blocking properties of the corresponding films while maintaining transparency in the visible region. Pang et al. (2016) demonstrated that cellulose membranes had good anti-UV properties after being modified by toluene diisocyanate (TDI) and 4,4′-dihydroxy benzophenone (DHBP). In our previous report, transparent NC-ZnO hybrid films for UV-blocking were successfully fabricated using sheet-like ZnO (s-ZnO) or belt-like ZnO (b-ZnO) nanostructures as additives (Jiang, Sun et al., 2015; Jiang, Song et al., 2015). Inheriting all the properties of low cost, nanoscale dimension, renewability, light weight and unique morphology, TEMPO-mediated oxidized nanocellulose (ONC) displays additional surface properties to easily combine a variety of functional materials (Bideau et al., 2016; Carlsson, Lindh, Nyholm, Strømme, & Mihranyan, 2014; Koga et al., 2013; Lin, Bruzzese, & Dufresne, 2012; Usov et al., 2015). Because of its numerous advantages, the potential application of ONC has also been utilized for preparing various special nanomaterials. For instance, Koga et al. (2013) prepared transparent, conductive and printable nanocomposites by mixing ONC with single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). Lin et al. (2012) reported a novel native alginate/cellulose sponges by crosslinking the well dispersed ONC with divalent calcium ions (Ca2+). Jiang, Zhao, Feng, Fang, and Shi (2016) synthesized transparent luminescent films using ONC grafted with carbon dots (CDs). Recently, CDs are emerging as a new class of carbon nanomaterials with board and strong UV absorbance (da Silva Souzaa, de Mesquitab, Lagoa, Caminhasa, & Pereira, 2016; Jiang, Sun et al., 2015; Junka, Guo, Filpponen, Laine, & Rojas, 2014; Zhu et al., 2015). Thus, it is obvious that CDs are prodigious potential combined with ZnO nanostructures for making a synergetic effect in enhancement UV-blocking.

Herein, we described a novel transparent ONC-ZnO film with enhanced UV-blocking functionalized with CDs via a pressured-extrusion process. Besides the above mentioned s-ZnO and b-ZnO, commercial particulate ZnO (p-ZnO) was also used to assemble with CDs-ONC in order to the future large-scale and cost-effective production of UV-blocking films. The effect of CDs incorporating different morphological ZnO on the simultaneous optical transparency and UV-blocking properties of ONC-CDs-ZnO films was investigated in details. The UV-blocking and visible-transparent NC films with high thermal stability will be widely used for vast application such as car windshields, clean windows, contact lenses, outdoor UV-sensitive polymers and some special biological test containers.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Bamboo was got from market in shanghai. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) radical, Zinc acetate dehydrate (Zn(OAc)2·2H2O), Urea, Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), Triton X-100, Acetic acid, Polyvinyl Pyrrolidone, Sodium bromide (NaBr), Trisodium phosphate (Na3PO4), Sodium silicate (Na2SiO3) and Nitric acid (HNO3) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium Chlorite (NaClO2) and ZnO nanoparticles (p-ZnO) were purchased from Aladdin. 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

Nanocellulose (NC) were isolated from bamboo fibers according to our previously reported method (Cao et al., 2015), and then the obtained NC was oxidized by applying TEMPO oxidation (Jiang et al., 2016). Sheet-like ZnO (s-ZnO) and belt-like ZnO (b-ZnO) nanostructures were synthesized according to our previous reports (Jiang, Sun et al., 2015; Jiang, Song et al., 2015).

CDs-ONC was synthesized via a one-step microwave procedure according to the previous reports (Liu et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2015). In brief, 0.2mL of TTDDA were added to the homogenized 10.0g of ONC suspensions (2.14wt%) and transferred into a 50mL autoclave, then the mixture was reacted at 600W and heated at 200°C for 2min in a microwave reaction system (MDS-6, Sineo Microwave Chemical Technology Co. Ltd.). After that, CDs-ONC nanocomposites were ultimately obtained by centrifugation and washing for several cycles.

CDs-ONC-ZnO films were fast assembled with an aqueous pressured-extrusion process (Song et al., 2015). 1.23g CDs-ONC composites were added to 80mL distilled water, and mixed with different concentration of s-ZnO, b-ZnO and commercial p-ZnO, respectively, to make a homogeneous solution under ultrasonication using a high frequency ultrasonic cell disruptor (High Nova instrument Co., Ltd., China). The homogeneous dispersion was poured into the extruder (NanoAble-150, PhD Technology LLC, USA) to squeeze out the excess water. After extrusion, the wet films were carefully peeled off from the PC filter membrane, sandwiched between two pieces of glass sheets and then dried at 85°C for 10min. CDs-ONC-ZnO films marked as CDs-ONC-s-ZnOx, CDs-ONC-p-ZnOx and CDs-ONC-b-ZnOx (x=0, 1, 2, 3, 4wt%) by changing the doping amount of ZnO. All the as-obtained films were measured as 30μm in thickness and 45mm in diameter.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-200CX, JEOL, Japan) was employed to represent the morphologies of NC and ONC at 120kV of accelerated voltage. A field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (JSM-6700F, JEOL, Japan) was applied to carry out the morphologies of the CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films. The absorbance and transmittance of hybrid films was measured with a UV–vis (2501PC, SHIMADZU, Japan) spectrophotometer at a scanning range of 200–800nm by investigating their UV-blocking properties. NETZSCH STA409PC (Simultaneous Thermal Analyzer) instrument was used to study the thermal stability of hybrid film samples, measuring at a heating rate of 10°C/min in the range of 30–800°C under the condition of air atmosphere. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a 3 KW D/MAX2200 V PC diffractometer using Cu kα radiation (60kV, 80mA) at a step width of 8°min−1. The structure ONC, CDs-ONC and CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films were investigated through fourier transform infrared spectroscopy in the spectral range from 4000 to 400cm−1 with Thermo Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) by using the pressed potassium bromide (KBr) discs as transparent pellets.

NC was isolated from bamboo fibers and subsequently surface oxidized by TEMPO radical. It is obvious that the ONC displays a dense interconnected fibrous network before homogenization process (Fig. 1 a). After being high-pressure homogenized, the resultant ONC has a plurality of uniformly distributed nanofibrils with appropriately 20–30nm in width and 1–2μm in length (Fig. 1b), which can be suitable for the light modulation of transparent films (Koga et al., 2013). CDs-ONC composites were synthesized via a microwave pyrolysis using TTDDA as carbon source. It can be clearly observed that CDs were successfully attached on the surface of ONC. The ONC still remains fiber morphology (Fig. 1c) and the as-prepared CDs exhibit homogeneous spherical particles with an average diameter of 5nm (Fig. 1d).

Transparent UV-blocking ONC films was fabricated using CDs-ONC as fibrous network substrates and synchronously functionalized with different ZnO nanostructures, such as s-ZnO, b-ZnO and p-ZnO (ESI, Fig. S1). As depicted in Fig. 2 , the CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films with different ZnO nanostructures possess very high optical transmittance, and the background patterns of the three kinds of transparent films can be easily distinguished by naked eyes. Meanwhile, the three kinds of CDs-ONC-ZnO films display gradually decreases in optical transparency with the increasing amounts of ZnO nanostructures. It can be deduced from SEM analysis that the surface of the pure CDs-ONC film is quite smooth without the addition of ZnO nanostructures. However, with the increasing amount of ZnO nanostructures from 1wt% to 4wt%, the surface roughness gradually became visible on CDs-ONC-ZnOx films, indicating the accumulations of ZnO nanostructures on fibrous networks in which the forward light scattering was suppressed. Thus the transparency of CDs-ONC-ZnOx films was slightly decreased accompanied by the increasing amount of ZnO nanostructures. As shown in the cross-sectional morphologies of the CDs-ONC and CDs-ONC-ZnOx films (Fig. S2), it is clear that different ZnO nanostructures additions cause severe changes in the fracture structure, since the pure CDs-ONC film exhibits a homogeneous and compactly layered surface. On the other hand, the addition of ZnO nanostructures leads to a disordered stacking network.

XRD patterns of CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films further demonstrated that the ZnO nanostructures were successfully assembled on the CDs-ONC films. The XRD patterns of CDs-ONC-s-ZnO, CDs-ONC-b-ZnO and CDs-ONC-p-ZnO hybrid films with different amounts of ZnO nanostructures were presented in Fig. 3 , respectively. All the characteristic peaks at 2θ=16.7°, 22.7° and 35.0° are corresponding to (110), (200), and (040) planes of cellulose (Gu & Hsieh, 2015; Lu et al., 2015). Additionally, a broad characteristic peak (002) of CDs centered at about 22° is hardly to found (Zhao, Shi, Fang, & Feng, 2015; Zhu et al., 2013), which may be overlapped by the broader peak (2θ=22.7°) of cellulose. For CDs-ONC-p-ZnOx (x=1, 2wt%) films, with the addition of p-ZnO nanostructures, nearly all the characteristic peaks of the p-ZnO were observed and very consistent with the typical values of hexagonal ZnO (JPCDS card No. 36-1451) (Feng, Shi, Wang, & Ma, 2008). On the other hand, the XRD patterns for CDs-ONC-s-ZnOx and CDs-ONC-b-ZnOx (x=1, 2wt%) are quite similar, the most prominent peak at 31.8° is indexed to the (100) plane of ZnO crystal, while the pattern of b-ZnO shows only a small intensity. The results clearly indicate that the ZnO nanostructures possess preferential growth direction.

FTIR spectra were further employed to evaluate the chemical interactions in the CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films. As shown in Fig. 4 , the absorption bands at 3310, 2910, 1736cm−1 are the characteristic of OH, CH, CO stretching vibrations of ONC, respectively. The band at 1644cm−1 is indicative of the acylation reaction between CDs and ONC (Jiang et al., 2016). Furthermore, a new peak appeared at 1466cm−1 in the spectrum of the CDs-ONC compared to ONC, which may be ascribed to the stretching vibration of OCH2− group originated from DDTTA, further indicating the combination of CDs and ONC. Additionally, the absorption band at 428cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of CDs-ONC-b-ZnO4, CDs-ONC-s-ZnO4 and a CDs-ONC-p-ZnO4 films is the characteristic peak of ZnO nanostructures.

UV–vis spectrophotometer was employed to investigate the optical transmittance and absorbance of the CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films (Fig. 5 ). It is obvious that the CDs-ONC-s-ZnOx, CDs-ONC-b-ZnOx and CDs-ONC-p-ZnOx (x=0, 1, 2, 3, 4wt%) films have excellent UV-blocking ability, and the UV absorption band nearly covers the whole UV region (Fig. 5a–c). Interestingly, the CDs-ONC hybrid film exhibits better UV-blocking ability, superior to our previously reported neat NC film (Jiang, Sun et al., 2015; Jiang, Song et al., 2015), indicating that the CDs has a positive effect on UV-blocking. Furthermore, the addition of ZnO nanostructures leads to a sharp increase of UV-blocking ratio. With the increasing amounts of different ZnO nanostructures, a stronger absorption band can be observed accordingly. The optical transmittance of CDs-ONC-s-ZnOx, CDs-ONC-b-ZnOx and CDs-ONC-p-ZnOx (x=0, 1, 2, 3, 4wt%) films were investigated in Fig. 5d–f, a decreasing trend ranging from 300nm to 800nm is presented with the increasing amount of ZnO, which is in good agreement with the results from Fig. 2. It can be seen that the transmittance is higher than the recently reported values from polylactide/ZnO (Murariu et al., 2011), poly(l-lactide)/ZnO (Lizundia, Ruiz-Rubio, Vilas, & León, 2015), and cellulose nanocrystals/ZnO films (Lizundia, Urruchi, Vilas, & León, 2016).

In order to make it clear to understand their UV-blocking properties, the transmittance, visible-shielding ratio (VR) and UV-blocking ratio (UVR) of the three hybrid films were summarized in Table 1 . The VR and UVR at 300nm, 225nm and 550nm were evaluated by using Eq. (1) (Li, Zou, & Hou, 2011):
(1)
R(%)=(T 0 − T)/T 0
Where the transmittance of CDs-ONC and hybrid films was defined as T 0 and T, respectively. Similar to our previous work (Jiang, Sun et al., 2015; Jiang, Song et al., 2015), CDs-ONC-s-ZnO film shows better UV-blocking ability compared to CDs-ONC-b-ZnO and CDs-ONC-p-ZnO films. As shown in Table 1, the UVR of CDs-ONC-s-ZnO film with 4wt% s-ZnO at 300nm and 225nm is 92.74% and 98.99%, respectively, much higher than those of NC-s-ZnO film without CDs under the same amount of s-ZnO (86.87% at 300nm, 91.82% at 225nm). Similar results were also observed from CDs-ONC-b-ZnO hybrid films, further suggesting the enhancement of CDs in UV-blocking. CDs decrease the UV reflection of ZnO due to its intrinsic absorption characteristics (Nooralian et al., 2016). Most importantly, when adding 4wt% commercial p-ZnO, its UVR (90.91% at 225nm) is very close to the value of NC-s-ZnO film with the same amount of s-ZnO, leading to drastically reducing the cost of UV-blocking films in practical applications by replacing those complicated structural ZnO.

The self-photodegradation of the CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films is also another important feature for potential long-term application. When the CDs-ONC-s-ZnO2, CDs-ONC-b-ZnO2 and CDs-ONC-p-ZnO2 films were irradiated with UV excitation light for over 72h (Table S1). There is nearly no weight loss of the hybrid films was observed, indicating that the CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films possess good photo-stability. In overall, the results demonstrate that the CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films are of especial interest to be used for applications in severe environmental conditions, for example high temperatures processing or long-time exposure.

In summary, we successfully fabricated a novel CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid film via TEMPO-mediated oxidized NC (ONC) and its derivatives of CDs incorporating with different ZnO nanostructures. The CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films with s-ZnO, b-ZnO and commercial p-ZnO possess excellent UV-blocking properties, and the UV absorption band nearly covers the whole UV region, respectively. Interestingly, the newly-obtained CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films present better UV shielding effect compared to our previously designed NC-ZnO hybrid films. Noteworthily, when adding 4wt% commercial p-ZnO, its UVR is very close to the value of NC-s-ZnO film with the same amount of s-ZnO, leading to drastically reducing the cost of UV-blocking films in large-scale production. Moreover, the CDs-ONC-ZnO hybrid films exhibit excellent thermal stability and photo-stability. All in all, these promising hybrid films possessed both excellent UV-blocking ratio, high visible light transparency and great thermal stability were considered as a series of outstanding applications in severe environmental conditions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially sponsored by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (13ZR1415100, 15ZR1415100). We are also grateful to Instrumental Analysis & Research Center of Shanghai University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2017.01.030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
